abstract: Widespread groundwater contamination by per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) necessitates efficient removal strategies during drinking water treatment, particularly for increasingly prevalent short-chain compounds. This study evaluates the comparative performance of granular activated carbon (GAC) and various anion exchange (IEX) resins (both strong and weak base) through laboratory batch experiments and pilot-scale column tests at a PFAS-polluted waterworks. Results demonstrate that sorption affinity is strongly dependent on chain length and functional groups; for all materials, short-chain perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCA), such as PFBA and PFPeA, exhibited significantly lower affinities compared to long-chain analogs or short-chain sulfonic acids (PFSA). In column tests, GAC achieved sufficient removal of PFBA and PFPeA only over limited run times (6,000 and 11,000 bed volumes, respectively), whereas longer PFCA (C≥6) allowed for extended operation. While IEX media provided higher capacities than GAC for certain short-chain compounds, their higher material costs make them economically uncompetitive in single-use mode. However, weak base ion exchangers demonstrated the potential for circular operation through regeneration. While long-chain PFAS required ethanol-based mixtures for effective elution, short-chain compounds like PFBA could be recovered using standard aqueous regenerants. These findings suggest that IEX with regeneration and subsequent liquid-liquid separation offers a viable economic and technical alternative to GAC for managing complex PFAS profiles in drinking water.
